meta.model: claude-opus-4-8 (Claude Code weekly review, injected call_fn)

digests.narrative:
The washout he leaned into last week collected its outside validation. A Morgan Stanley CPO note independently named SIVE, COHR, and LITE as the laser-supply leaders — placing a ~$1.5B laser company beside two $60B+ names and confirming from sell-side coverage the three-leader framing he'd built from his own OSINT. He sharpened the differentiation that matters: AAOI is a pluggable-revenue business sampling lasers but with no CPO design wins and "TBD" commercial timing, and MTSI has effectively fallen off the CPO-laser map (Ayar removed Macom from its site) — so CPO-laser leadership narrows to SIVE/LITE/COHR, not the whole optical complex. Innolight's IR added a demand leg: 1.6T demand hasn't contracted and 800G was revised up "significantly," with neoclouds and AI-model companies diversifying the buyer base beyond hyperscalers and the module shortage described as broad.

Beneath the finished lasers he kept mapping the raw-material chokepoints — Nippon Chemical's red phosphorus (#1 globally, with Rasa) feeding AXTI, InP substrates into IQE/TSEM, and an MLCC/barium chain — the picks-beneath-the-picks that make the laser scarcity structural rather than cyclical. SIVE stays the sharpest single name: it trades on a thin Swedish listing that algos routinely misprice, but MS placing it next to LITE and COHR pulls it onto the institutional radar, and the prior week's insider-buy cluster still anchors the conviction.
keyPoints: Morgan Stanley CPO note independently named SIVE/COHR/LITE as the laser leaders — sell-side validation of his OSINT-built three-leader thesis
AAOI clarified as pluggable-only: sampling lasers but no CPO design wins, "TBD" timing — CPO-laser leadership narrows to SIVE/LITE/COHR
Innolight IR: 800G demand revised up "significantly," 1.6T intact; neoclouds + AI-model buyers diversify demand beyond hyperscalers
Raw-material chokepoints mapped deeper: Nippon Chemical red phosphorus → AXTI, InP → IQE/TSEM — makes the laser scarcity structural
SIVE the sharpest name: thin Swedish listing algos misprice, now on institutional radar after the MS placement
Conviction unchanged — the bear catalysts (delay reports, China-laser scare) were denied and are now being validated against

digests.narrative: The memory leg picked up hyperscaler confirmation this week: Meta's published internal memo shows long-term agreements signed with Samsung and SNDK for memory as part of a 7GW-this-year, doubling-in-2027 compute build — exactly the demand-durability argument the upcycle thesis needs. On the substrate side he reaffirmed AXTI ('still holding') with his ~40%-of-InP-supply framing, though the week's mentions were list-posts rather than new evidence; the confirmed anchor remains the 3-year Coherent wafer deal with a $22.3M prepayment and the Nomura ASP-hike schedule (2-inch InP +42-76%, 3-inch ~+78%). He also flagged a market-structure curiosity: SK Hynix set to open at a ~21% premium on its listing, wondering aloud whether Korea-index products gap with it.
keyPoints: Meta internal memo: memory LTAs signed with Samsung and SNDK — hyperscaler demand contracted, not spot
AXTI reaffirmed but on list-post momentum; Coherent 3-year deal ($22.3M prepay) is the hard anchor
Nomura ASP hikes: 2in InP +42-76%, 3in ~+78%, EML epiwafers +50-75%
SK Hynix listing premium flagged as a Korea-index market-structure event

digests.narrative:
The silicon-photonics foundry layer got externally mapped this week. Morgan Stanley's CPO note placed TSM, GFS, and TSEM as the silicon-photonics foundry capacity beneath the CPO laser leaders — the same "platform beneath his photonics book" frame he's held, now with a sell-side stamp. Tower Semi (TSEM) reinforced it with a concrete capacity announcement: a Japanese-government-supported expansion of 300mm silicon-photonics, SiGe, and advanced-packaging capacity, targeting $1.2B net profit and $3.6B revenue in 2028 (~23x 2028 forward P/E at a ~$28B market cap) — a fab putting capex behind the SiPho ramp rather than just being named in a note.

The durable specialty-foundry idea is unchanged and still the strongest standalone fab-layer thesis: XFAB as the cheapest specialty foundry with a free photonixfab silicon-photonics option, GlobalFoundries as the SCALE reference-laser platform where AMD's CPO reportedly routes, and the equipment names (ASML/AMAT/LRCX/KLAC) as the unavoidable layer beneath the whole stack. His cross-sector frame holds: when the tape sells the fab/equipment complex on macro leverage, sold-out SiPho and advanced-packaging capacity means the order books didn't move with the price.
keyPoints: Morgan Stanley mapped TSM/GFS/TSEM as the silicon-photonics foundry capacity beneath the CPO laser leaders — sell-side stamp on his platform frame
Tower Semi (TSEM): Japan-backed 300mm SiPho/SiGe/advanced-packaging expansion, targeting $3.6B revenue / $1.2B net profit in 2028
Capacity is being put behind the SiPho ramp, not just named — capex validation of the foundry-layer thesis
Durable idea intact: XFAB cheapest specialty foundry (+ free photonixfab option); GFS the SCALE reference-laser platform for AMD's CPO
Equipment names (ASML/AMAT/LRCX/KLAC) remain the unavoidable layer — they trade with macro routs even when order books don't move

digests.narrative:
The 'Meta is cutting capex' narrative he has fought for weeks broke down publicly: Meta's upcoming 'Watermelon' model has reportedly caught up to OpenAI's GPT 5.5 using an order of magnitude more compute than its predecessor, and SemiAnalysis — the same firm whose bear notes hit photonics — says Meta's datacenter and compute will accelerate with 2027 capex 'shockingly high.' His long-standing read (a compute-constraint story, never a demand-weakening one) is aging well, and NBIS remains his highest-conviction pure-play expression of it.

The Anthropic demand wave became this week's concrete catalyst: TeraWulf signed a $19B datacenter lease with Anthropic ('a very positive tailwind for the Neocloud/Colo sector'), and leaked Anthropic docs point to ~1.4GW of Australian capacity worth ~$21.6B — mapping directly onto the sovereign datacenters IREN, SHAZ and other neoclouds have been building there (he discloses no positions in those two). The more speculative fringe — floating/ocean-based datacenters as a future NBIS-style play — he treats as directional color, not thesis.
keyPoints: Meta capex-cut narrative debunked: 'Watermelon' reportedly matches GPT 5.5 on an order of magnitude more compute; SemiAnalysis sees 2027 Meta capex 'shockingly high.'
TeraWulf signs a $19B datacenter lease with Anthropic — a repricing event for the whole neocloud/colo sector's contract value.
Leaked Anthropic docs point to ~1.4GW / ~$21.6B of Australian capacity, mapping onto sovereign DCs built by IREN, SHAZ and peers.
NBIS remains his highest-conviction pure-play neocloud; the demand chain is now corroborated from hyperscalers and frontier labs simultaneously.
Floating/ocean-based datacenters are flagged as an emerging concept (free cooling, geographic constraints) — color, not thesis.

digests.narrative:
Lighter-touch theme, mostly picks-and-shovels research. AEHR's ~100% rally is viewed as largely priced in, with limited near-term re-rating room absent a new catalyst. LPKF (LPK.DE) is a recurring bullish idea on its glass-core substrate monopoly — >80% of major global players selecting its equipment for validation, a 70% market-share target, and NASDAQ-listing discussions — with a stated $3-5B fair value at full volume ramp. Soitec (SOI.PA) is repeatedly cited as a European chokepoint SOI-wafer supplier benefiting from EU CHIPS Act 2.0's new photonics-policy priority, alongside LPKF and XFAB.

ASE Technology (ASX) and Teradyne (TER) round out the packaging/test layer, mentioned inside broader '$10-100B AI-exposed names' baskets as offering compelling ROI versus larger names. Ongoing trade-record research (Starlink and other direct-supplier mapping) continues to surface obscure materials names, and the China->Japan->Korea tungsten (WF6) export-control thread remains a recurring materials-bottleneck watch item.
keyPoints: LPKF (LPK.DE): glass-core substrate monopoly, >80% major-player validation, 70% market-share target, NASDAQ-listing discussions — stated $3-5B fair value at full volume ramp.
Soitec (SOI.PA): European SOI-wafer chokepoint supplier, a repeated beneficiary of EU CHIPS Act 2.0's new photonics-policy priority.
AEHR's ~100% rally is seen as mostly priced in — limited near-term re-rating room without a new catalyst.
ASE Technology (ASX) and Teradyne (TER) round out the packaging/test layer, cited inside broader AI-exposed-name baskets for compelling ROI.
The China->Japan->Korea tungsten (WF6) export-control thread remains a recurring materials-bottleneck watch item.

digests.narrative: The Marvell thread stays sentiment-first: whenever MRVL drops he hears Jensen's 'the next $1T company' comment, and this week he formalized his half-serious 'conspiracy theory' — Nvidia quietly supports the ASIC challengers (MRVL, MediaTek, AlChip, GUC) chipping away at Broadcom's custom-silicon share, the same way neocloud offerings built a moat against CSP custom ASICs last year. He frames it as an interesting ~2-year trade but expects hyperscalers like Google to bring design in-house past 2030, and floats NVDA taking a stake in QCOM next. Nokia recurs as a validating evaluator (with NVDA) of XFAB's silicon-photonics foundry. Still no standalone networking bottleneck thesis — this remains the most narrative-driven category in the corpus.
keyPoints: Formalized 'conspiracy theory': NVDA discretely backs ASIC challengers (MRVL, MediaTek, AlChip, GUC) against Broadcom's custom-silicon share.
Framed as a ~2-year trade opportunity — past 2030 he expects hyperscalers like Google to bring ASIC design in-house.
Jensen's 'next $1T company' comment still anchors MRVL sentiment through drawdowns; he predicts NVDA takes a stake in QCOM next.
Nokia (with NVDA) continues validating XFAB's silicon-photonics foundry for CPO applications.
Still no standalone networking bottleneck thesis — mentions remain list-driven and narrative-first.

digests.narrative: A new, thin entry in this cycle: Corning (GLW) surfaced only inside a broader Bloomberg-driven semis-selloff basket (down ~11.4% alongside AAOI/MRVL/LITE/SNDK/TER on a report UBS called 'not new news'). No dedicated glass-substrate thesis has been posted on it yet — it's tracked here as a natural candidate for the glass-core advanced-packaging wave (alongside SKC Absolics/TSMC CoPoS) given Corning's position as the leading specialty-glass and optical-fiber maker, but that connection hasn't been stated directly by the analyst.
keyPoints: Corning (GLW) has only appeared inside a broader semis-selloff basket so far — no dedicated glass-substrate thesis posted yet.
Tracked as a natural fit for the glass-core advanced-packaging wave given Corning's specialty-glass/fiber leadership, though the analyst hasn't drawn that link explicitly.

digests.narrative: The theme escalated from research to declared maximum conviction: 'I'm personally trying to own as much % of Agility Robotics as possible right now,' at a ~$3.9B pre-money valuation via the CCXI SPAC, brushing off SPAC-history objections. His sharpened frame is geopolitical: China ships ~90% of the world's humanoid robots (Elon's comment: 'for now'), which makes a US champion politically valuable — and he positions Agility as the Made-in-America leader with a RoboFab targeting 10K+ units/year and 75%+ US-sourced components, versus Tesla's clearer mass-production path but China-heavy BOM. The desk notes this is his most talking-his-book theme: real assets and real commercialization partners (Amazon, MercadoLibre), but euphoria-phase framing throughout.
keyPoints: Declared intent to maximize Agility ownership at ~$3.9B pre-money via the CCXI SPAC
China ships ~90% of humanoids — US-champion scarcity is the new thesis leg
Agility RoboFab: 10K+ units/year capacity, 75%+ US-sourced components
TSLA framed as the mass-production path but with China-dependent sourcing

digests.narrative:
The one genuinely new accelerator signal was Jensen Huang stepping out personally to do damage control on the delay reports. He said 800V and rack-to-rack optical interconnects are progressing in line with the original plan with no material delay, debunked the rumor that Rubin Ultra slips to 2028 (says it ships next year), and framed the Kyber-replacement rack redesign as an architectural optimization rather than a schedule hit — while claiming no ASIC market-share loss. For this map that matters most as a read-through to photonics: the CEO of the demand center directly denying the optical-interconnect delay that triggered the sector selloff is the same catalyst reversal the laser names are trading on.

Otherwise the accelerator layer stays where his edge is thinnest — NVDA appears mostly as the gravitational center whose roadmap sets the tape for every supplier beneath it, plus the still-explicitly-speculative theory that Nvidia quietly backs ASIC challengers (MRVL, MediaTek, AlChip) against Broadcom. He owns the theme through the suppliers this map surfaces, not the megacap chips themselves.
keyPoints: Jensen personally denied the delay reports: 800V + rack-to-rack optical interconnects "in line with plan, no material delay"
Rubin Ultra 2028-delay rumor debunked (ships next year); Kyber-replacement redesign framed as architectural optimization, not a slip
Claimed no ASIC market-share loss — a direct rebuttal to the SemiAnalysis-style supply-chain bear framing
Read-through to photonics: the demand center denying the optical-interconnect delay is the catalyst the laser names are trading on
Edge still thinnest here: NVDA as the gravitational center; the "Nvidia backs ASIC challengers vs Broadcom" theory stays speculative

digests.narrative: His 'Meta is not cutting' thesis won the week outright. A published internal Meta memo showed AI ramping as usual — LTAs with Samsung and SNDK for memory and Sumitomo Electric for fiber, ~7GW of compute deployed this year and expected to double in 2027, up to $145B capex, and a new chip every six months through 2027 — followed days later by a new $10B datacenter in Canada. 'So much for the media framing of Meta Compute as overbuilding and cutting capex.' The rest of the week's hyperscaler talk was narrative texture: OpenAI antagonizing every Mag7 partner it has (TSLA, MSFT, NVDA, AAPL) while building its own ecosystem, which he reads admiringly as the industry's genuine disruptor dynamic, and xAI's Grok 4.5 reportedly reaching frontier quality at lower cost.
keyPoints: Meta internal memo: up to $145B capex, 7GW compute this year doubling in 2027, chip every 6 months
New $10B Meta datacenter in Canada — directly contradicts the capex-cut framing
Memory + fiber LTAs (Samsung, SNDK, Sumitomo) contract the supply chain his other themes ride
OpenAI-vs-Mag7 and Grok 4.5 read as healthy frontier competition, not demand risk
